extra.message: User input from request.POST['user_id'] is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to User.objects.raw(). An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via the user_id POST parameter (e.g., ' OR '1'='1). The comment in the code explicitly acknowledges this: 'which allows sql injection'.

cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

extra.message: Both the 'col' parameter (column selector) and the 'ip' parameter (from request.GET['ip']) are interpolated directly into a raw SQL string via string formatting. In admin/views.py the 'col' is constructed from unsanitized GET parameter keys and 'ip' comes directly from request.GET. This allows SQL injection via both the column name and IP filter. No parameterized query or escaping is used.

cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

extra.message: User-controlled input from request.GET['class'] is passed directly to eval(). An attacker can supply any Python expression or class name, resulting in arbitrary code execution on the server. No authentication, allowlist, or sanitization is applied before the eval() call.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')

extra.message: Same as the api_index endpoint: user-controlled input from request.GET['class'] is passed directly to eval() in api_id without any sanitization or authentication check.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')

extra.message: User-supplied POST parameter 'user' is base64-decoded and then passed to pickle.loads(). An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload and submit it in the password reset form, resulting in arbitrary code execution. The encoded user object is passed through the client via an HTML form field and can be tampered with.

cwe: CWE-502: Deserialization of Untrusted Data

extra.message: The 'name' GET parameter is appended directly to BASE_DIR to construct a file path without any sanitization or path normalization. An attacker can supply a value like '../../etc/passwd' or '../pygoat/settings.py' to read arbitrary files on the server. The resulting path is opened and returned as a file download.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: The uploaded file's name (orig_file.name) is concatenated into a shell command string passed to os.system(). Since the filename originates from the user-supplied upload, an attacker can craft a filename containing shell metacharacters (e.g., 'file; rm -rf /') to execute arbitrary OS commands. The code comment explicitly states 'intended vulnerability for command injection'.

cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')

extra.message: The 'path' parameter from request.POST is used directly as the redirect target in HttpResponseRedirect(path) after successful login. An attacker can craft a login form with path='https://evil.com' to redirect the victim to an arbitrary external URL after authentication, enabling phishing attacks.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: The work_info view fetches user data using the user_id from the URL path parameter directly via User.objects.get(id=user_id) without verifying that the authenticated user owns the requested record. Any authenticated user can access another user's work information (including SSN) by changing the user_id in the URL. The test file test_a4_insecure_direct_object_reference.py explicitly confirms this vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The user_pay DELETE endpoint fetches a Pay record using pay_id from the URL without checking whether the Pay record belongs to the currently authenticated user. Any authenticated user can delete any other user's pay/direct deposit records by guessing or enumerating pay_id values.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The user_message view fetches a Message using message_id from the URL without verifying that the authenticated user is either the creator or receiver of the message. Any authenticated user can view or delete any message by guessing or enumerating message_id values.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: All admin endpoints (admin_dashboard, admin_get_user, admin_delete_user, admin_update_user, admin_get_all_users, admin_analytics) are only protected by user_is_authenticated, which only checks for a valid auth_token cookie. There is no check that the authenticated user has is_admin=True. Any regular authenticated user can access admin functionality including listing all users, deleting users, and updating user records.

cwe: CWE-862: Missing Authorization

extra.message: Passwords are hashed using MD5 (hashlib.md5) without any salt. MD5 is a fast, cryptographically broken hash algorithm unsuitable for password storage. An attacker who obtains the database can crack all passwords efficiently using rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force. The same pattern appears in hash_password() at line 133.

cwe: CWE-916: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

extra.message: Authentication tokens are generated using MD5 of (email + random integer in range 1..1,000,000). This is predictable: MD5 is not a CSPRNG, and only 1,000,000 possible values exist per user. An attacker who knows a user's email can brute-force all possible auth tokens offline and forge sessions.

cwe: CWE-330: Use of Insufficiently Random Values

extra.message: The auth_token session cookie is set without the 'httponly' or 'secure' flags. Without httponly=True, the cookie is accessible to JavaScript and can be stolen via XSS. Without secure=True, the cookie is transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, enabling interception.

cwe: CWE-1004: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag

extra.message: The safe_name() method wraps self.first_name with Django's mark_safe(), which tells the template engine not to escape the value. If the first_name field contains HTML or JavaScript (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>), it will be rendered unescaped in the browser, resulting in stored XSS wherever safe_name() is called in templates.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: safestring.mark_safe() is called with a hardcoded HTML string in creator_name() when the creator is not found. While the hardcoded string itself is safe, using mark_safe on dynamic data in surrounding code is a pattern that can introduce XSS. More critically, mark_safe is used as a fallback return value making it easy to accidentally call mark_safe on user-controlled content in this pattern.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as a literal string in the settings file. The SECRET_KEY is used to sign cookies, sessions, CSRF tokens, and password reset tokens. If an attacker obtains this key (e.g., via source code disclosure), they can forge any of these security primitives.

cwe: CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials

extra.message: The AES encryption key (KEY) used to encrypt sensitive values like SSNs and bank account numbers is hardcoded as a literal byte string in settings.py. Any developer or attacker with access to the source code can decrypt all encrypted sensitive data.

cwe: CWE-321: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key

extra.message: ACCESS_TOKEN_SALT used to sign API access tokens is hardcoded as a literal string. An attacker with this value can forge valid API tokens for any user_id.

cwe: CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials

extra.message: DEBUG=True is set in the main settings file. In DEBUG mode Django returns detailed error pages including stack traces, local variable values, and SQL queries, which discloses sensitive internal information to attackers.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code
CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: ALLOWED_HOSTS is set to ['*'], which permits the application to be accessed via any HTTP Host header. This can facilitate HTTP Host header injection attacks and disables Django's host validation protection.

cwe: CWE-16: Configuration

extra.message: Database password 'password' is hardcoded in the Docker settings file for the MySQL root user. This weak, hardcoded credential is committed to version control and would give an attacker full database access if obtained.

cwe: CWE-259: Use of Hard-coded Password

extra.message: The client IP address is extracted from the X-Forwarded-For header without validation. This header is user-controlled and can be spoofed. Because this IP is stored in the analytics table and later used as a filter parameter in SQL queries (Analytics.hits_by_ip), a spoofed IP containing SQL syntax can contribute to SQL injection in the analytics endpoint.

cwe: CWE-290: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing

extra.message: Password reset tokens are generated as user_id + '-' + MD5(email). MD5 is cryptographically broken, and the token is entirely deterministic (no random component). An attacker who knows any user's email and user_id (both potentially guessable or leaked) can generate a valid password reset token without receiving the email, allowing account takeover.

cwe: CWE-640: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password

extra.message: Multiple user accounts with plaintext passwords are hardcoded in the seed command: admin@metacorp.com/admin1234, jack@metacorp.com/yankeessuck, jim@metacorp.com/alohaowasp, mike@metacorp.com/motocross1445, ken@metacorp.com/citrusblend. These credentials are committed to version control and may be used in production deployments.

cwe: CWE-259: Use of Hard-coded Password

extra.message: The SSN field is stored as a plain CharField in the database alongside the encrypted_ssn BinaryField. Even when encrypt_ssn() is called, SSN is only set to None in the Python object but the unencrypted value may remain committed in the database depending on whether save() is called after encryption. Additionally, seed.py creates work_info records with plaintext SSNs without calling encrypt_ssn(). SSNs are also displayed in templates without authorization checks (confirmed by the IDOR test).

cwe: CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
